metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete exhaustion log message template to the 'Retry observability' section that explicitly includes the `llm transport retry` prefix (e.g. `llm transport retry: exhausted after <n> attempts — <exc>`), so the AC7 caplog assertion has a single authoritative target for both the before_sleep and exhaustion paths.
findings.finding: The AC7 observability assertion requires caplog to match the `llm transport retry` prefix for BOTH retry attempts AND exhaustion, but the 'Retry observability' section only pins that prefix to the `before_sleep` callback (retry attempts). The exhaustion log site — where `reraise` surfaces and the plan says 'exhaustion is logged where reraise surfaces' — is never given a concrete log message format or the `llm transport retry` prefix. An implementer writing the exhaustion logger and the test asserting it have no single authoritative spec for the exhaustion log line's prefix/format.
findings.location: Plan section 'Retry observability'; AC line 7: 'the AsyncClient does not leak ... an ADR stub exists at the placeholder path; Retry attempts + exhaustion are logged at WARNING on the rebar.llm.runner logger with a stable llm transport retry prefix ... asserted via caplog matching that substring'
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Retry observability' section, add: 'Exhaustion is logged at WARNING with the same prefix: `llm transport retry: exhausted after <n> attempts: <exc>`'. Update AC7 to quote this template explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the 'Retry observability' section, explicitly state that the exhaustion log (emitted after `reraise` surfaces, e.g. in a `except` block in `runner.py`) ALSO uses the `llm transport retry` prefix so AC8's caplog assertion is unambiguously satisfiable.
findings.finding: AC8 asserts caplog will match the `llm transport retry` prefix for BOTH retry attempts AND exhaustion, but the What/observability section only specifies that prefix for the `before_sleep` callback; the exhaustion log is described only as occurring 'where `reraise` surfaces' with no specified prefix — leaving the AC assertion underspecified and potentially unverifiable as written.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Retry observability subsection: 'The exhaustion catch-site in `PydanticAIRunner.run()` also logs at WARNING with the same `llm transport retry` prefix: `llm transport retry: exhausted after <n> attempts — <exc>`; this is the log line AC8's caplog assertion matches on the exhaustion path.'
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen the ADR AC to require the stub contains at minimum: the decision statement, the rationale (side-effect-safety / agent-loop restart risk), and the key alternatives considered — not just file existence.
findings.finding: The ADR AC is gated only on file existence ('an ADR stub exists at the placeholder path'), not on minimum required content. A nearly-empty file satisfies the AC, yet the plan's stated rationale — 'retry at the httpx transport with SDK max_retries=0, justified by side-effect-safety' — is the load-bearing decision other stories (hoopoe, blackbear) must understand. If the stub ships with just a title, the architectural decision is not actually captured for future maintainers.
findings.location: AC line: 'an ADR stub exists at the placeholder path' / Section: ADR
findings.suggested_fix: Revise the AC to: 'An ADR stub exists at the placeholder path and contains at minimum: (1) Status/Context, (2) Decision (transport-layer retry, SDK max_retries=0), (3) Rationale (side-effecting tool calls must not be replayed), (4) Alternatives considered (application-level retry, message-history replay).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address the interaction between the existing `model_settings['timeout']` (runner.py:285-286) and the new `httpx.Timeout(cfg.timeout_s)` on the `AsyncClient` — clarify in the plan which setting governs per-attempt timeout on the Anthropic path after this story, and whether the model_settings timeout should be suppressed for Anthropic when the AsyncClient already carries one.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `httpx.Timeout(cfg.timeout_s)` on the new `httpx.AsyncClient` for the Anthropic path, but the existing `runner.py:285-286` also sets `model_settings['timeout'] = float(cfg.timeout_s)`, which pydantic-ai passes to its own request timeout. On the Anthropic path after this story, both timeout settings will be applied simultaneously — the plan is silent on which wins and whether they conflict.
findings.location: "Timeout semantics + the hoopoe merge interface" section; runner.py:278-286

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify in the plan (and implement accordingly) whether `model_settings["timeout"]` at `runner.py:285-286` is retained, removed, or superseded by the new `httpx.Timeout(cfg.timeout_s)` on the AsyncClient, to avoid two competing timeout mechanisms targeting the same underlying request.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'every existing LLM OPERATIONAL knob (`timeout_s`, `max_tokens`, `max_iterations`) already lives on `LLMConfig`' but the existing timeout is ALSO set via `model_settings["timeout"]` at `runner.py:285-286`. The plan introduces a second timeout surface (`httpx.Timeout(cfg.timeout_s)` on the AsyncClient) without specifying whether the existing `model_settings["timeout"]` is retained, removed, or superseded — leaving two concurrent timeout mechanisms on the same underlying client after this story lands.
findings.location: Plan section 'Timeout semantics + the hoopoe merge interface'; runner.py:278-286

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan (and in the exhaustion AC test) that on retry exhaustion, callers of `PydanticAIRunner.run()` receive `LLMUnavailableError` (wrapping the original httpx error via `__cause__`), not the raw `httpx.HTTPStatusError` — update the AC assertion accordingly.
findings.finding: The existing `except Exception` catch-all in `runner.py:353-365` wraps every non-`LLMError` exception — including the `httpx.HTTPStatusError` that `reraise=True` surfaces on exhaustion — into `LLMUnavailableError`. The plan's AC states 'the exhaustion path re-raises the original exception', but that is only true at the transport level; callers of `PydanticAIRunner.run()` will receive `LLMUnavailableError`, not the raw httpx error.
findings.location: AC line: 'a test sets `llm_retry_max_attempts=1` and asserts NO retry occurs (fail-fast opt-out), and the exhaustion path (all attempts fail) re-raises the original exception'; runner.py:353-365

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the two cfg.timeout_s application points: specify whether the pre-existing `model_settings['timeout']` line (runner.py:285-286) should be removed once the `httpx.Timeout(cfg.timeout_s)` per-attempt bound is set on the AsyncClient, and explicitly document/assert that cfg.timeout_s is a per-attempt cap (retried up to N times) rather than a total-operation wall-clock cap — ensuring the operator's timeout intent is preserved.
findings.finding: After this story, cfg.timeout_s is applied at TWO places: (1) the pre-existing model_settings['timeout'] path (runner.py:285-286), which pydantic-ai passes as an httpx per-request timeout, AND (2) the new httpx.Timeout(cfg.timeout_s) on the AsyncClient. Because httpx.TimeoutException is in the transport-retry predicate, a slow-but-legitimate call that hits the per-attempt timeout will be retried up to llm_retry_max_attempts times, potentially multiplying worst-case wall-clock by the full attempt count. The plan describes httpx.Timeout as 'per-attempt' but does not address whether the pre-existing model_settings['timeout'] path should be removed, or how the two interact — leaving the operator's cfg.timeout_s semantics ambiguous (per-attempt cap vs. total-operation cap) and the retry-on-timeout behavior unspecified.
findings.location: Plan section 'Timeout semantics + the hoopoe merge interface'; runner.py:285-286; AC: 'The unified client always has a per-attempt httpx.Timeout'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Elevate the `except` branch of the `anyio.run(http_client.aclose)` try/finally to log at WARNING (not DEBUG) so aclose failures are visible in default-level production logs, and add the AC: 'if aclose raises, a WARNING is emitted on `rebar.llm.runner`'.
findings.finding: The `httpx.AsyncClient.aclose()` failure path is logged at DEBUG only, providing no visible signal to operators in default-level production logs if the connection pool fails to close cleanly.
findings.location: Notes on prior art / AsyncClient lifecycle section: 'the `except` logs at DEBUG (not fully silent) and never fails the run'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the except handler to `log.warning('llm transport client close failed: %s', exc)` and add a test that patches `aclose` to raise and asserts a WARNING is captured by caplog.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `anyio` is importable as a direct dependency in the rebar `agents` extra (add it explicitly to `pyproject.toml` if not already present), OR replace `anyio.run(http_client.aclose)` with an alternative teardown path (e.g. a synchronous `httpx.Client` wrapper, or `asyncio.get_event_loop().run_until_complete(...)`) that uses only confirmed direct deps.
findings.finding: The plan's `AsyncClient` teardown mechanism uses `anyio.run(http_client.aclose)` ('VERIFIED offline'), but `anyio` is not imported anywhere in the rebar codebase and is not a declared direct dependency in the `agents` extra of `pyproject.toml` — it is only a transitive dep of pydantic-ai. The in-rebar usage is unverifiable by grep/read.
findings.location: Plan section 'Notes on prior art — AsyncClient lifecycle (concrete)'; pyproject.toml:99-111
findings.suggested_fix: Add `anyio>=3` to the `agents` extra in `pyproject.toml`, or use `import asyncio; asyncio.run(http_client.aclose())` which is stdlib and does not depend on a third-party package.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `anyio` to the `agents` extra in `pyproject.toml` (or replace `anyio.run(http_client.aclose)` with a stdlib-compatible alternative such as `asyncio.run(http_client.aclose())`) — the dependency must be declared or the implementation changed to not require it.
findings.finding: `anyio` is used in the plan's proposed implementation (`anyio.run(http_client.aclose)`) but is not a dependency in the `agents` extra or any other `pyproject.toml` extra.
findings.suggested_fix: Either add `'anyio>=4'` to the `agents` extra list in `pyproject.toml`, or change the teardown to use `asyncio.run(http_client.aclose())` which is available in the stdlib (Python ≥3.11 is already the runtime floor).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_optional.py` to the plan's Files list (or explicitly note it) and update the `EXTRAS['agents']` description string to reflect `pydantic-ai-slim[anthropic,retries]`.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/_optional.py` `EXTRAS['agents']` contains a description string that names `pydantic-ai-slim[anthropic]` explicitly. After the dependency string changes to `pydantic-ai-slim[anthropic,retries]`, this description becomes stale. The file is not listed in the plan's Files section.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the description in EXTRAS['agents'] from `pydantic-ai-slim[anthropic]` to `pydantic-ai-slim[anthropic,retries]` (tenacity-backed transport retry).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a targeted spike probe that explicitly inspects `RetryConfig` constructor params (e.g. `inspect.signature(RetryConfig)` or `RetryConfig.__init__.__code__.co_varnames`) and confirms `before_sleep` is accepted — then update the experiment log with the actual result before AC7 is written.
findings.finding: The plan labels `RetryConfig(..., before_sleep=cb)` as VERIFIED in the 'Retry observability' section, but the experiment log never explicitly confirms `before_sleep` as an accepted kwarg of `pydantic_ai.retries.RetryConfig`. The log only shows `AsyncTenacityTransport params: ['config', 'wrapped', 'validate_response']` — the transport's constructor, not `RetryConfig`'s fields. In tenacity 9.x, `before_sleep` is a param of `tenacity.Retrying`/`AsyncRetrying` directly; whether pydantic-ai's `RetryConfig` wrapper passes it through (or whether it must be set on a manually constructed `AsyncRetrying` passed as `RetryConfig.retry`) is unverified in the spike output.
findings.location: What / Retry observability section; AC7 ('before_sleep callback on the RetryConfig (VERIFIED: RetryConfig(..., before_sleep=cb) is accepted)')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the experiment log: `[7] RetryConfig params: inspect.signature(pydantic_ai.retries.RetryConfig).parameters.keys()` — if `before_sleep` is absent, use `tenacity.AsyncRetrying(before_sleep=cb, ...)` and pass it via the `RetryConfig` field that accepts a tenacity retrying object.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `RetryConfig` parameter introspection to the experiment log (e.g. `print('RetryConfig params:', list(inspect.signature(RetryConfig.__init__).parameters))`) and capture the output to confirm `before_sleep` is an accepted kwarg; if it is not, document the alternative hook (e.g. tenacity's native `before_sleep` kwarg on the underlying `Retrying` object) and update the plan and AC accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan claims 'RetryConfig(..., before_sleep=cb) is accepted (VERIFIED)' for the observability callback, but the experiment log only introspects AsyncTenacityTransport params=['config','wrapped','validate_response'] — no RetryConfig parameter introspection is captured anywhere in the logged output. If pydantic-ai's RetryConfig is its own wrapper class (distinct from tenacity's Retrying), its accepted kwargs are a separate, unverified surface. A TypeError at construction time would break every agent run; a silently-dropped kwarg would break the observability AC without a test failure.
findings.location: Plan section 'Retry observability'; 'Verified library behavior' experiment log [3]
findings.suggested_fix: In the spike or an inline verification, add: `import inspect; from pydantic_ai.retries import RetryConfig; print('RetryConfig params:', list(inspect.signature(RetryConfig.__init__).parameters))`; then update the experiment log with the captured output.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `pydantic_ai.retries.AsyncTenacityTransport` and `wait_retry_after` are part of pydantic-ai's documented public API stability contract for the `[retries]` extra (check pydantic-ai's CHANGELOG and/or `__all__` exports); if they are not guaranteed stable, add an import smoke test that runs in `make test` (offline, no model needed) so a version bump that moves the symbol fails CI rather than a live run.
findings.finding: The plan imports from `pydantic_ai.retries` (an internal module path, not a declared public API surface in pydantic-ai's stability contract) and pins `>=1,<2`. The spike verifies this path at 1.107.0 only. If a future 1.x minor reorganizes `pydantic_ai.retries` (e.g. moves `AsyncTenacityTransport` or `wait_retry_after` to a different submodule), the import fails silently in CI until a live run triggers the lazy import inside `PydanticAIRunner.run()`.
findings.location: Dependency section; 'The single unified Anthropic construction' section; pyproject.toml:108

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a captured output line to the spike experiment log specifically showing `anyio.run(http_client.aclose)` completing without error after `agent.run_sync()` (e.g. `print('aclose: ok')`), and confirm the no-unclosed-client-warning test runs under the same anyio backend as production; alternatively, document using `http_client.aclose()` inside the same `run_sync` coroutine or via a synchronous `httpx.Client` close to avoid the nested-anyio-run question.
findings.finding: The plan states `anyio.run(http_client.aclose)` closes the httpx.AsyncClient pool cleanly after `agent.run_sync()` and marks this 'VERIFIED offline,' but the experiment log's captured output only shows request counts and output strings — no aclose teardown line is captured. `agent.run_sync()` itself uses anyio internally; calling `anyio.run()` a second time in the same thread immediately afterward creates a new event loop, which can conflict with some anyio backends or leave the prior loop's cleanup incomplete. If the teardown silently fails the plan's `except` swallows it at DEBUG level, and the no-unclosed-client-warning AC could pass on the specific backend used in CI while failing in production.
findings.location: Plan section 'AsyncClient lifecycle (concrete)'; 'Notes on prior art' bullet; AC line 7 ('AsyncClient does not leak')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly list `REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_ATTEMPTS` and `REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_WAIT_S` in the plan's config-keys section as required additions to the `_clean` fixture's `delenv` list (not just in the AC), so the implementer has a co-located processing rule alongside the schema definition.
findings.finding: The plan mandates (AC8) that `test_config_llm.py`'s `_clean` fixture list the two new env vars `REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_ATTEMPTS` and `REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_WAIT_S`. Inspection of the existing `_clean` fixture (test_config_llm.py lines 28-44) confirms neither env var is present. The plan names this as an AC deliverable but provides no implementation spec for WHICH env var name to add or HOW the fixture must be extended — the two new env names are mentioned only in the config key names section, not cross-referenced to the fixture's delenv list.
findings.location: AC line 8: 'test_config_llm.py's _clean fixture lists them'; test_config_llm.py lines 28-44
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Config keys' section, add a note: 'Add both REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_ATTEMPTS and REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_WAIT_S to the _clean fixture delenv list in test_config_llm.py alongside the other REBAR_LLM_* vars.'

findings.finding: The `_build_retrying_anthropic_model` helper's `http_timeout: httpx.Timeout | None = None` parameter and the guarantee that a non-None `httpx.Timeout(cfg.timeout_s)` default is always applied (so there is 'ALWAYS a per-attempt bound, never an unbounded stall') is described as a concrete deliverable in the 'Timeout semantics + the hoopoe merge interface' section and is named as part of this story's owned construction seam — but no AC verifies the helper signature, the default-timeout behavior, or that the per-attempt bound is present when hoopoe has not yet supplied an `httpx.Timeout`.
findings.location: 'Timeout semantics + the hoopoe merge interface' section; AC list (no matching item)
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the AC list: '`_build_retrying_anthropic_model` uses `httpx.Timeout(cfg.timeout_s)` when `http_timeout` is `None` (asserted: the `AsyncClient` constructed by the helper carries a non-None timeout when no explicit `http_timeout` is supplied); the helper signature is stable for the hoopoe merge.'

findings.finding: The retry-observability AC ('Retry attempts + exhaustion are logged (asserted via caplog)') does not specify the log message pattern or logger name the caplog assertion should match. The `before_sleep` callback is described as logging 'attempt number + the wait it is about to sleep' but no concrete message format is given. An implementer writing the caplog assertion must invent the format, meaning the AC cannot be evaluated as written without a clarifying choice.
findings.location: AC item 7: 'Retry attempts + exhaustion are logged (asserted via caplog)'; 'Retry observability' section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to 'Retry observability': 'The before_sleep callback logs at WARNING under the `rebar.llm.runner` logger with a message matching `"LLM retry attempt {n}"` (attempt number) and `"waiting {w}s"` (sleep duration); exhaustion is logged at ERROR with `"LLM retries exhausted after {n} attempts"`. Tests assert caplog contains these substrings at the specified levels.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
